content.full_text:
August 23, 1977


W. E. White
14009 San Antonio Dr.
Norwalk, CA 90650

Dear Mr. White:

Thank you for your letter of July 30, 1977 regarding cam-
paign disclosure requirements of the Political Reform Act.

I will respond to your questions in the order posed:

1. The Political Reform Act requires that the source of cam-
paign contributions must be identified. Since the definition of
"contribution" includes the term "payments" which in turn means
"loans", a candidate must report in his campaign statement the
source of loans of $50 or more received for political purposes.
The Commission in 2 Cal Adm. Code Section 18215 has determined
that "political purposes" means made for the purpose of influencing
or attempting to influence or influence the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates. . .".

2. All loans of $50 or more used in the campaign, whether or
not secured, received from other than a commercial lending insti-
tution for political purposes, must be disclosed.

3. Any person, other than a commercial lending institution,
who lends $3,000 or more in a calendar year for political purposes
is a committee Government Code Section 82013(e) and is re-
quired to file a major donor's campaign statement on Form 440, a
copy of which is enclosed.

4. A person who makes a non-monetary contribution of goods or
services to candidates or campaign committees totaling $500 or more
in a calendar year is a committee under Government Code Section
82013(b) and is required to file campaign statements on Form
460, a copy of which is enclosed.

W. E. White                    -2-                    August 23, 1977


     5. The recipient of a non-monetary contribution must report
the fair market value of the goods or services, which is the amount
that would have to be paid to acquire similar goods or services on
the open market.

     I hope this information is helpful. If you would like further
assistance, please contact our staff again. Enclosed is a copy of
our manual which describes in some detail the campaign disclosure
reporting requirements along with regulation 18215 referred to above.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Nan Emmerson
                                        Special Compliance
                                        Representative

NE/gt

Enclosures

July 30, 1977


Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 667
Sacramento, Calif. 95814


Gentlemen:

1. Does a candidate have to report in his campaign statement the source
of a loan when the money is used in his campaign?

2. Does there have to be any entry on the campaign statement to reflect
a loan made by the general chairman of a "Committee to Elect _____" 
if the loan is secured by a mortgage?

3. Does the lender in question #2 required to make any report to the FPPC
when he knows that the proceeds of the loan will go towards the
campaign expenses?  (lender was the general chairman of the committee)

4. Is there any requirement that a non-monetary contributor make any report
to the FPPC?  (as example, if the contribution exceeds say $1,000.)

5. Is there any violation when the non-monetary contributor is listed as
donating the value of say $200.00 per month as rental value of an office,
when the asking price was $400.00 per month to anyone else?


Sincerely,


W. S. White
14309 San Antonio Dr.,
Norwalk, Calif. 90650

July 8, 1977


MR. W. L. WHITE
1400 SAN ANTONIO AVE.
NORWALK, CALIFORNIA

Dear Mr. White:

Thank you for the letter on the July 5 regarding copies
of campaign statements submitted to
the Secretary of State. We will arrange for reply.
Should you wish additional copies of campaign statements,
write to the Secretary of State, Political Reform Division,
P.O. Box 1469, [illegible]

Sincerely,


[illegible] AVMSAGER
Political Assistance and
Amortiz Division
